author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
Of the Number of Natures in the Manichæan Fiction.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Of the Number of Natures in the Manichæan Fiction.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Again, I wish to know, when I read of God the Father and His kingdoms founded on the shining and happy region, whether the Father and His kingdoms, and the region, are all of the same nature and substance.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If they are, then it is not another nature or sort of body of God which the wedge of the race of darkness cleaves and penetrates, which itself is an unspeakably revolting thing, but it is actually the very nature of God which undergoes this.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Think of this, I beseech you: as you are men, think of it, and flee from it; and if by tearing open your breasts you can cast out by the roots such profane fancies from your faith, I pray you to do it.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Or will you say that these three are not of one and the same nature, but that the Father is of one, the kingdoms of another, and the region of another, so that each has a peculiar nature and substance, and that they are arranged according to their degree of excellence?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If this is true, Manichæus should have taught that there are four natures, not two; or if the Father and the kingdoms have one nature, and the region only one of its own, he should have made three.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Or if he made only two, because the region of darkness does not belong to God, in what sense does the region of light belong to God?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For if it has a nature of its own, and if God neither generated nor made it, it does not belong to Him, and the seat of His kingdom is in what belongs to another.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Or if it belongs to Him because of its vicinity, the region of darkness must do so too; for it not only borders on the region of light, but penetrates it so as to sever it in two.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Again, if God generated it, it cannot have a separate nature.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For what is generated by God must be what God is, as the Catholic Church believes of the only begotten Son.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: So you are brought back of necessity to that shocking and detestable profanity, that the wedge of darkness sunders not a region distinct and separate from God, but the very nature of God.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Or if God did not generate, but make it, of what did He make it?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Or if of Himself, what is this but to generate?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If of some other nature, was this nature good or evil?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If good, there must have been some good nature not belonging to God; which you will scarcely have the boldness to assert.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If evil, the race of darkness cannot have been the only evil nature.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Or did God take a part of that region and turn it into a region of light, in order to found His kingdom upon it?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If He had, He would have taken the whole, and there would have been no evil nature left.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If God, then, did not make the region of light of a substance distinct from His own, He must have made it of nothing.
